title: One Edit Away
description: Return YES if s can become t with at most one insert/delete/replace.

examples.explanation: Step 1: Generate replace edits from 'cat'. Replace c at position 0: 'aat', 'bat', 'cat', 'dat'... (all 26 letters). Replace a at position 1: 'cbt', 'cct', 'cdt'... Replace t at position 2: 'caa', 'cab', 'cac'... Step 2: Generate delete edits. Delete c: 'at'. Delete a: 'ct'. Delete t: 'ca'. Step 3: Generate insert edits. Insert at position 0: 'acat', 'bcat', 'ccat'... Insert at position 1: 'caat', 'cbat', 'ccat'... Insert at position 2: 'cata', 'catb', 'catc'... Insert at position 3: 'cata', 'catb', 'catc'... Step 4: Check if 'bat' exists in generated set. Found in replace set (replace 'c' with 'b' gives 'bat'). Return YES.

Step 1: Generate replace edits from 'dog'. Replace d: 'aog', 'bog', 'cog'... Replace o: 'dag', 'dbg', 'dcg'... Replace g: 'doa', 'dob', 'doc'... Step 2: Generate delete edits. Delete d: 'og'. Delete o: 'dg'. Delete g: 'do'. Step 3: Generate insert edits. Insert at position 0: 'adog', 'bdog', 'cdog'... Insert at position 1: 'daog', 'dbog', 'dcog'... Insert at position 2: 'doag', 'dobg', 'docg'... Insert at position 3: 'doga', 'dogb', 'dogc'... (including 'dogs' when inserting 's' at position 3). Step 4: Check if 'dogs' exists. Found in insert set. Return YES.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * m)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible strings by applying one edit (insert, delete, or replace) to s, then check if any match t. This is straightforward but inefficient for longer strings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — One Edit Away with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution checking if string s can become t with at most one edit by generating all possible one-edit strings from s.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the 'One Edit Away' problem by exhaustively exploring all possible single edits to string s and checking if any result matches string t. Intuitively, this works because we're directly implementing the problem definition: we generate every string that's exactly one edit away from s, then see if t appears in that set. The algorithm has three phases: 1) Generate all strings by replacing each character in s with every possible character (26 for lowercase English letters), 2) Generate all strings by deleting each character from s, 3) Generate all strings by inserting each possible character at every position in s. We then check if t exists in this combined set. The time complexity is O(n * m) where n is length of s and m is the alphabet size (typically 26), plus O(n) for deletions and O((n+1)*m) for insertions. Space complexity is O(n * m) to store all generated strings. This connects to L1 by using the three edit generation functions as building blocks. Common mistakes include forgetting edge cases (empty strings, identical strings), inefficient string concatenation, and not handling all three edit types. Real code pattern: generate sets for each edit type, union them, check membership.

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate replace edits from 'cat'. Replace c at position 0: 'aat', 'bat', 'cat', 'dat'... (all 26 letters). Replace a at position 1: 'cbt', 'cct', 'cdt'... Replace t at position 2: 'caa', 'cab', 'cac'... Step 2: Generate delete edits. Delete c: 'at'. Delete a: 'ct'. Delete t: 'ca'. Step 3: Generate insert edits. Insert at position 0: 'acat', 'bcat', 'ccat'... Insert at position 1: 'caat', 'cbat', 'ccat'... Insert at position 2: 'cata', 'catb', 'catc'... Insert at position 3: 'cata', 'catb', 'catc'... Step 4: Check if 'bat' exists in generated set. Found in replace set (replace 'c' with 'b' gives 'bat'). Return YES.

Step 1: Generate replace edits from 'dog'. Replace d: 'aog', 'bog', 'cog'... Replace o: 'dag', 'dbg', 'dcg'... Replace g: 'doa', 'dob', 'doc'... Step 2: Generate delete edits. Delete d: 'og'. Delete o: 'dg'. Delete g: 'do'. Step 3: Generate insert edits. Insert at position 0: 'adog', 'bdog', 'cdog'... Insert at position 1: 'daog', 'dbog', 'dcog'... Insert at position 2: 'doag', 'dobg', 'docg'... Insert at position 3: 'doga', 'dogb', 'dogc'... (including 'dogs' when inserting 's' at position 3). Step 4: Check if 'dogs' exists. Found in insert set. Return YES.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All One-Edit Strings
ladders.desc: Generate all possible strings that are exactly one edit away from a given string.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, generating every string that can be obtained by applying exactly one edit (replace, delete, or insert) to the input string s. Intuitively, we need to explore the entire neighborhood of strings reachable with one edit operation. The algorithm works in three parts: 1) Replace: For each position i in s (0 to n-1), replace s[i] with every character in the alphabet (assuming lowercase English letters, so 26 possibilities). This generates n * 26 strings. 2) Delete: For each position i, remove s[i] to get a string of length n-1. This generates n strings. 3) Insert: For each position i from 0 to n (n+1 positions), insert every alphabet character at that position. This generates (n+1) * 26 strings. We combine all these into a set to avoid duplicates. Time complexity is O(n * m) where n is string length and m is alphabet size (26). Space complexity is O(n * m) to store the generated strings. This connects to L0 by providing the complete set of candidate strings to check against t. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in insertion positions, not handling empty strings correctly, and inefficient string building. Real code pattern: initialize empty set, three nested loops for each edit type, add to set.

examples.trace: Step 1: Replace edits. Position 0: replace 'a' with 'a' to 'z' gives 'ab', 'bb', 'cb'... (26 strings). Position 1: replace 'b' with 'a' to 'z' gives 'aa', 'ab', 'ac'... (26 strings). Step 2: Delete edits. Delete at position 0: 'b'. Delete at position 1: 'a'. Step 3: Insert edits. Insert at position 0: 'aab', 'bab', 'cab'... (26 strings). Insert at position 1: 'aab', 'abb', 'acb'... (26 strings). Insert at position 2: 'aba', 'abb', 'abc'... (26 strings). Total: 26+26+2+26+26+26 = 132 strings in set.

Step 1: Replace edits. Position 0: replace 'a' with 'a' to 'z' gives 'a', 'b', 'c'... (26 strings). Step 2: Delete edits. Delete at position 0: '' (empty string). Step 3: Insert edits. Insert at position 0: 'aa', 'ba', 'ca'... (26 strings). Insert at position 1: 'aa', 'ab', 'ac'... (26 strings). Total: 26+1+26+26 = 79 strings in set.

testCases.expected: Set with 26 strings from insert at position 0
Set containing '' and all two-letter strings starting or ending with 'a'
Set containing 'a', 'b', and all three-letter strings with 'ab' as substring
Set containing all strings where changing one character gives 'xyz'
Set assuming alphabet includes digits (adjust accordingly)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Edit Operation Generation
ladders.desc: Generate strings by applying a single edit operation (replace, delete, or insert) systematically.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the systematic generation of strings through individual edit operations. The core insight is that each edit operation can be decomposed into simple string manipulations. For replace: we need to change exactly one character while keeping all others unchanged. This requires iterating through each position and substituting with each possible character. For delete: we remove exactly one character, which means taking the substring before and after that position. For insert: we add exactly one character at any position, including before the first character and after the last. The intuition is that these three operations cover all possible ways to change a string with exactly one edit. The algorithm for each operation: 1) Replace: for i in range(len(s)): for char in alphabet: new_string = s[:i] + char + s[i+1:]. 2) Delete: for i in range(len(s)): new_string = s[:i] + s[i+1:]. 3) Insert: for i in range(len(s)+1): for char in alphabet: new_string = s[:i] + char + s[i:]. Time complexity for each operation is O(n * m) for replace/insert, O(n) for delete. Space complexity is O(n * m) for storing results. This connects to L1 by being the implementation of each edit type. Common mistakes include incorrect slice indices, not handling empty strings in delete/insert, and not considering all insertion positions. Real code pattern: use string slicing and concatenation in nested loops.

examples.trace: Step 1: Position 0. s[:0]='', s[1:]='at'. Replace with 'a': ''+'a'+'at'='aat'. Replace with 'b': 'bat'. Replace with 'c': 'cat'. ... through 'z'. Step 2: Position 1. s[:1]='c', s[2:]='t'. Replace with 'a': 'c'+'a'+'t'='cat'. Replace with 'b': 'cbt'. ... Step 3: Position 2. s[:2]='ca', s[3:]=''. Replace with 'a': 'caa'. Replace with 'b': 'cab'. ... Total 78 strings (3*26).

Step 1: Position 0. s[:0]='', s[0:]='dog'. Insert 'a': 'adog'. Insert 'b': 'bdog'. ... through 'z'. Step 2: Position 1. s[:1]='d', s[1:]='og'. Insert 'a': 'daog'. Insert 'b': 'dbog'. ... Step 3: Position 2. s[:2]='do', s[2:]='g'. Insert 'a': 'doag'. Insert 'b': 'dobg'. ... Step 4: Position 3. s[:3]='dog', s[3:]=''. Insert 'a': 'doga'. Insert 'b': 'dogb'. ... Total 104 strings (4*26).

testCases.expected: Combined set of all three operations

ladders.title: Building Block — String Manipulation with Slicing
ladders.desc: Create new strings by modifying parts of an existing string using slicing and concatenation.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves manipulating strings by extracting substrings and combining them with new characters. String slicing allows us to access portions of a string without modifying the original. The intuition is that we can build new strings by taking the parts before and after a modification point, then inserting the change in between. For example, to replace a character at index i, we take s[0:i] (characters before i), add the new character, and append s[i+1:] (characters after i). Similarly, deletion removes the character at i by concatenating the before and after parts. Insertion adds a character at position i by taking s[0:i], adding the new character, and appending s[i:]. The algorithm uses Python-style slicing where s[start:end] gives characters from start to end-1, and omitting start/end defaults to beginning/end. Time complexity for each operation is O(n) for creating the new string. Space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanical ability to perform edit operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in slice indices, not understanding that slicing creates new strings, and inefficient repeated concatenation. Real code pattern: use + operator to concatenate slices and characters.

examples.trace: Step 1: Get substring before index 2: s[0:2] = 'he'. Step 2: Get substring after index 2: s[3:] = 'lo' (since s[2] is 'l', we want after it). Step 3: Concatenate: 'he' + 'x' + 'lo' = 'hexlo'. Verification: Original 'hello' -> positions: 0:h,1:e,2:l,3:l,4:o. Replace position 2 (l) with x gives h,e,x,l,o = 'hexlo'.

Step 1: Get substring before index 3: s[0:3] = 'wor'. Step 2: Get substring after index 3: s[4:] = 'd' (since s[3] is 'l', we want after it). Step 3: Concatenate: 'wor' + 'd' = 'word'. Verification: Original 'world' -> positions: 0:w,1:o,2:r,3:l,4:d. Delete position 3 (l) gives w,o,r,d = 'word'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing and Concatenation
ladders.desc: Extract substrings and combine strings using fundamental operations.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two key string manipulations: slicing to get parts of a string, and concatenation to join strings together. Slicing uses the syntax s[start:end] to get characters from index start up to but not including end. If start is omitted, it defaults to 0; if end is omitted, it defaults to the end of the string. Negative indices count from the end. Concatenation uses the + operator to join two or more strings into a new string. The intuition is that strings are immutable sequences of characters, so any modification requires creating a new string. The algorithm for slicing: determine start and end indices, extract the substring. For concatenation: use + to combine strings in order. Time complexity for slicing is O(k) where k is the length of the slice; for concatenation, it's O(n+m) for strings of length n and m. Space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This connects to L3 by providing the tools to implement string modifications. Common mistakes include confusing slice indices (end is exclusive), forgetting that slicing creates a copy, and inefficient concatenation in loops. Real code pattern: s[:i] gets prefix, s[i:] gets suffix, use + to join.

examples.trace: Step 1: String 'programming' has indices: 0:p,1:r,2:o,3:g,4:r,5:a,6:m,7:m,8:i,9:n,10:g. Step 2: Slice s[3:7] takes characters from index 3 up to but not including 7. Step 3: Indices 3,4,5,6 are g,r,a,m. Result: 'gram'.

Step 1: String s1 is 'hello', length 5. Step 2: String s2 is 'world', length 5. Step 3: Concatenate using +: 'hello' + 'world' = 'helloworld'. Step 4: New string has length 10, characters: h,e,l,l,o,w,o,r,l,d.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings as Sequences
ladders.desc: Understand strings as ordered collections of characters accessible by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces strings as sequences of characters where each character has a position called an index. In programming, strings are typically immutable, meaning they cannot be changed after creation. The intuition is that a string like 'cat' is stored as three characters: 'c' at index 0, 'a' at index 1, and 't' at index 2. We can access individual characters using square brackets, e.g., s[0] gives 'c'. The length of a string is the number of characters, obtained with len(s). This is crucial because edit operations depend on knowing positions and lengths. The algorithm for basic operations: 1) Access: use s[i] to get character at index i. 2) Length: use len(s) to get number of characters. 3) Iteration: use a loop to go through each character. Time complexity for access is O(1), for length is O(1) in most languages. Space complexity is O(n) to store the string. This connects to L4 by providing the basis for slicing and concatenation. Common mistakes include indexing out of bounds (e.g., s[3] for 'cat'), confusing 0-based indexing, and not understanding immutability. Real code pattern: treat strings like lists of characters.

examples.trace: Step 1: String 'hello' has length 5. Step 2: s[0] is 'h'. Step 3: s[1] is 'e'. Step 4: s[2] is 'l'. Step 5: s[3] is 'l'. Step 6: s[4] is 'o'. Step 7: s[5] would cause an error (index out of bounds). Step 8: len(s) returns 5.
Step 1: String 'abc' has indices 0,1,2. Step 2: Loop for i in range(len(s)): i=0, char=s[0]='a'. Step 3: i=1, char='b'. Step 4: i=2, char='c'. Step 5: End of loop.

approaches.name: Two Pointer with Early Exit O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to traverse s and t simultaneously, allowing at most one mismatch. If lengths differ, simulate an edit by adjusting pointers. This avoids extra space and stops early on failure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — One Edit Away with Two Pointer Early Exit
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if string s can become t with at most one edit using two pointers and early exit.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the complete 'One Edit Away' problem using the two-pointer approach with early exit. The intuition is that we can traverse both strings simultaneously with pointers i and j, allowing at most one mismatch. If the strings have different lengths, we simulate an edit by adjusting the pointer for the longer string when a mismatch occurs. The algorithm stops early if more than one edit would be required, making it efficient. We first check if the length difference exceeds 1, which would require more than one edit. Then we traverse with two pointers, tracking mismatches. When characters differ, if lengths are equal, we treat it as a replacement and advance both pointers. If lengths differ, we treat it as an insertion/deletion by advancing only the pointer of the longer string. If a second mismatch occurs, we return NO immediately. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the length of the longer string, as we traverse at most once. Space complexity is O(1) since we use only pointers and a counter. This connects to L1 by implementing the full logic that uses the mismatch handling subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling length differences correctly or forgetting to advance pointers properly after a mismatch. Real code pattern: def one_edit_away(s, t): if abs(len(s)-len(t)) > 1: return 'NO'; i=j=0; mismatch=False; while i<len(s) and j<len(t): if s[i]!=t[j]: if mismatch: return 'NO'; mismatch=True; if len(s)==len(t): i+=1; j+=1; elif len(s)>len(t): i+=1; else: j+=1; else: i+=1; j+=1; return 'YES'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Lengths 4 and 3, difference=1 ≤1, continue. Step 2: i=0,j=0: s[0]='p', t[0]='p', match, i=1,j=1. Step 3: i=1,j=1: s[1]='a', t[1]='l', mismatch, mismatch=False so set mismatch=True, len(s)>len(t) so i=2 (simulate delete 'a' from s). Step 4: i=2,j=1: s[2]='l', t[1]='l', match, i=3,j=2. Step 5: i=3,j=2: s[3]='e', t[2]='e', match, i=4,j=3. Step 6: Loop ends, return YES.

Step 1: Lengths both 4, difference=0 ≤1, continue. Step 2: i=0,j=0: s[0]='p', t[0]='b', mismatch, mismatch=False so set mismatch=True, lengths equal so i=1,j=1 (replace). Step 3: i=1,j=1: s[1]='a', t[1]='a', match, i=2,j=2. Step 4: i=2,j=2: s[2]='l', t[2]='k', mismatch, mismatch=True so return NO early.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Handle Single Mismatch with Length Adjustment
ladders.desc: Implement the core logic to handle one character mismatch considering string lengths.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pointer approach, handling what happens when we encounter a mismatch between s[i] and t[j]. The intuition is that a single edit can be a replacement (if lengths are equal) or an insertion/deletion (if lengths differ). When a mismatch occurs, we check if it's the first mismatch; if not, we fail immediately. If it is the first, we determine the edit type: for equal lengths, we advance both pointers (simulating replacement); for different lengths, we advance only the pointer of the longer string (simulating insertion into the shorter or deletion from the longer). This ensures we correctly account for the edit without needing to modify the strings. Time complexity is O(1) per call, as it performs constant-time checks and pointer updates. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called within the main traversal loop. Common mistakes include advancing the wrong pointer or not updating the mismatch flag. Real code pattern: def handle_mismatch(s, t, i, j, mismatch): if mismatch: return False, i, j; mismatch=True; if len(s)==len(t): i+=1; j+=1; elif len(s)>len(t): i+=1; else: j+=1; return True, i, j.

examples.trace: Step 1: mismatch=False, so set mismatch=True. Step 2: len(s)=4, len(t)=3, len(s)>len(t), so advance i only: i=2, j stays 1. Return updated values.
Step 1: mismatch=False, so set mismatch=True. Step 2: len(s)=4, len(t)=4, lengths equal, so advance both: i=1, j=1. Return updated values.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two Pointer Traversal with Mismatch Counting
ladders.desc: Traverse two strings simultaneously using pointers, counting mismatches up to one.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using two pointers, i for string s and j for string t, to iterate through both strings in sync. The intuition is that by moving pointers forward together when characters match, and handling mismatches specially, we can detect if more than one edit is needed without comparing all possible edits. We initialize pointers at 0 and a mismatch flag as False. In a loop while both pointers are within bounds, if s[i] equals t[j], we advance both pointers. If they differ, we check the mismatch flag: if already True, we stop and return failure (since that would be a second edit); if False, we set it to True and handle the mismatch based on string lengths (as detailed in L1). After the loop, we return success since at most one mismatch occurred. Time complexity is O(min(len(s), len(t))) in the worst case, but O(n) overall. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the mismatch handling subroutine. Common mistakes include not checking pointer bounds correctly or infinite loops. Real code pattern: i=j=0; mismatch=False; while i<len(s) and j<len(t): if s[i]==t[j]: i+=1; j+=1; else: if not handle_mismatch(...): return 'NO'; return 'YES'.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0,j=0: 'c'=='c', match, i=1,j=1. Step 2: i=1,j=1: 'a'!='u', mismatch, mismatch=False so set mismatch=True, lengths equal so i=2,j=2 (replace). Step 3: i=2,j=2: 't'=='t', match, i=3,j=3. Step 4: Loop ends, return YES.

Step 1: i=0,j=0: 'c'=='c', match, i=1,j=1. Step 2: i=1,j=1: 'a'=='a', match, i=2,j=2. Step 3: i=2,j=2: 't'=='t', match, i=3,j=3. Step 4: i=3 out of bounds for s (len=3), j=3 within t (len=4), loop ends. Since mismatch=False, return YES (t has extra 's', which is one insert).

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare Strings with Allowed Length Difference
ladders.desc: Check if two strings can be compared allowing for a length difference of at most one.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves preprocessing to determine if two strings can potentially be one edit away based solely on their lengths. The intuition is that if the lengths differ by more than 1, it's impossible to make them equal with just one edit (since each edit changes length by at most 1). For example, if s has length 5 and t has length 3, the difference is 2, which would require at least two inserts or deletes. Thus, we compute the absolute difference between len(s) and len(t). If it is greater than 1, we can immediately return NO without further processing. If it is 0 or 1, we proceed with the two-pointer traversal. This step optimizes the algorithm by providing an early exit. Time complexity is O(1) for the length check. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by being the first step before pointer traversal. Common mistakes include forgetting to use absolute value or misinterpreting the length condition. Real code pattern: if abs(len(s)-len(t)) > 1: return 'NO'.

examples.trace: Step 1: len(s)=3, len(t)=4, difference=|3-4|=1 ≤1, so proceed to pointer traversal.
Step 1: len(s)=3, len(t)=5, difference=|3-5|=2 >1, so return NO immediately.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through Strings with Pointers
ladders.desc: Use pointers to traverse two strings simultaneously, comparing characters.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves setting up two index pointers, typically i and j, to iterate through strings s and t. The intuition is that by moving pointers forward step-by-step, we can compare corresponding characters. We start both pointers at 0. In a loop, we check if i is less than len(s) and j is less than len(t) to ensure we don't go out of bounds. Inside the loop, we compare s[i] and t[j]. If they are equal, we increment both pointers to move to the next characters. If they differ, we handle the mismatch (as in higher levels). This operation forms the basis of the two-pointer technique. Time complexity depends on the loop iterations, up to O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for the pointers. This connects to L3 by being the mechanism used after the length check. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in pointer increments or not checking bounds. Real code pattern: i=0; j=0; while i<len(s) and j<len(t): # compare s[i] and t[j]; i+=1; j+=1.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0,j=0: compare 'a' and 'a', match, i=1,j=1. Step 2: i=1,j=1: compare 'b' and 'c', mismatch handled elsewhere. End loop as i=2,j=2 out of bounds.
Step 1: i=0,j=0: compare 'c' and 'd', mismatch. Step 2: After handling, loop may continue or exit based on mismatch count.

testCases.expected: i=0,j=0 match, i=1,j=1 match, i=2,j=2 end
i=0,j=0 match, i=1,j=1 match, i=2,j=2 mismatch
i=0,j=0 match, i=1,j=1 match, i=2,j=2 match, i=3,j=3 match, i=4,j=4 out of bounds for t
i=0,j=0 match, i=1,j=1 match, i=2 out of bounds for s
i=0,j=0 match, i=1,j=1 match, i=2,j=2 mismatch
No iteration as i out of bounds immediately

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Length Basics
ladders.desc: Understand how to access characters in a string and get its length.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the problem. A string is a sequence of characters. In programming, we can access individual characters using indices, starting from 0. For example, if s = 'hello', then s[0] is 'h', s[1] is 'e', etc. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains, obtained with len(s) in Python-like languages. For 'hello', len(s) is 5. It's crucial to remember that the last index is len(s)-1, so accessing s[5] would be out of bounds. This foundation is needed because the two-pointer approach relies on comparing s[i] and t[j] and checking i < len(s) to avoid errors. The intuition is that strings are ordered, so we can step through them position by position. Time complexity for accessing a character or getting length is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for these operations. This connects to L4 by providing the basic tools for pointer traversal. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or forgetting that strings are immutable. Real code pattern: char = s[index]; length = len(s).

examples.trace: Step 1: len(s)=3. Step 2: s[0]='c', s[1]='a', s[2]='t'. Step 3: s[3] is invalid (out of bounds).
Step 1: len(s)=0. Step 2: s[0] is invalid (out of bounds since length is 0).

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming Edit Distance O(n * m)
approaches.summary: Compute the full edit distance between s and t using DP, then check if it's at most 1. This generalizes to any number of edits but is overkill for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — One Edit Away with DP Edit Distance
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP to compute edit distance and check if ≤1
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the 'One Edit Away' problem by computing the full edit distance between strings s and t using dynamic programming, then checking if the distance is at most 1. The edit distance (Levenshtein distance) measures the minimum number of single-character edits (insertions, deletions, or substitutions) required to transform s into t. We build a DP table dp[i][j] representing the edit distance between the first i characters of s and first j characters of t. The recurrence relation is: if s[i-1] == t[j-1], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (no edit needed); otherwise, dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j] (delete from s), dp[i][j-1] (insert into s), dp[i-1][j-1] (replace)). After filling the table, we check if dp[n][m] ≤ 1. This approach generalizes to any number of edits but has O(n*m) time and space complexity, which is optimal for the general edit distance problem but overkill for checking just one edit. The intuition is that we systematically explore all possible edit sequences through overlapping subproblems, with the DP table storing optimal solutions to subproblems. Connection to L1: This uses the DP table computation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, misunderstanding the recurrence relation, or not initializing the base cases correctly (dp[i][0] = i, dp[0][j] = j). Real code pattern: def one_edit_away(s, t): n, m = len(s), len(t); dp = [[0]*(m+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; for i in range(n+1): dp[i][0] = i; for j in range(m+1): dp[0][j] = j; for i in range(1, n+1): for j in range(1, m+1): if s[i-1] == t[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]); return dp[n][m] <= 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Table Computation for Edit Distance
ladders.desc: Compute the full edit distance DP table between two strings
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the edit distance (Levenshtein distance) between two strings s and t using dynamic programming. It creates a 2D DP table dp where dp[i][j] represents the minimum edit distance between the first i characters of s and first j characters of t. The algorithm initializes base cases: dp[i][0] = i (deleting all i characters from s to match empty t) and dp[0][j] = j (inserting j characters into empty s to match t). Then it iterates through i from 1 to n and j from 1 to m. For each pair (i,j), if s[i-1] equals t[j-1], no edit is needed at this position, so dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise, we consider three possible edits: 1) Delete s[i-1]: cost = 1 + dp[i-1][j]; 2) Insert t[j-1] into s: cost = 1 + dp[i][j-1]; 3) Replace s[i-1] with t[j-1]: cost = 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]. We take the minimum of these three options. The intuition is that we build up solutions to larger subproblems from smaller ones, exploiting overlapping subproblems. Time complexity is O(n*m) as we fill an n+1 by m+1 table. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the table. Connection to L0: This computes the dp table that L0 uses to check if distance ≤1. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (off-by-one), forgetting base cases, or misapplying the recurrence. Real code pattern: def edit_distance_dp(s, t): n, m = len(s), len(t); dp = [[0]*(m+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; for i in range(n+1): dp[i][0] = i; for j in range(m+1): dp[0][j] = j; for i in range(1, n+1): for j in range(1, m+1): if s[i-1] == t[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]); return dp.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation for Edit Distance
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the recurrence relation for edit distance
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation that defines how to compute dp[i][j] based on smaller subproblems. For strings s and t, with dp[i][j] as edit distance between s[0:i] and t[0:j], the relation is: if s[i-1] == t[j-1], then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (characters match, no edit needed). If they differ, dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]). Here, dp[i-1][j] corresponds to deleting s[i-1] (cost 1) then matching s[0:i-1] with t[0:j]; dp[i][j-1] corresponds to inserting t[j-1] into s (cost 1) then matching s[0:i] with t[0:j-1]; dp[i-1][j-1] corresponds to replacing s[i-1] with t[j-1] (cost 1) then matching the rest. The intuition is that to transform s[0:i] to t[0:j], we consider the last characters: if they match, we just need to transform the prefixes; if not, we pick the cheapest among three edit operations. This recurrence exhibits optimal substructure: the optimal solution to the larger problem depends on optimal solutions to subproblems. Connection to L1: This recurrence is used to fill the DP table in L1. Common mistakes include confusing the indices (using i,j instead of i-1,j-1 for characters), misunderstanding which operation corresponds to which dp value, or not adding 1 for the edit cost. Real code pattern: if s[i-1] == t[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]).

examples.trace: We have s[1]='b', t[1]='c', they differ. Compute min of: dp[i-1][j]=dp[1][2]=1 (delete 'b'), dp[i][j-1]=dp[2][1]=1 (insert 'c'), dp[i-1][j-1]=dp[1][1]=0 (replace 'b' with 'c'). min is 0, so dp[2][2]=1+0=1.
We have s[2]='t', t[2]='t', they match. So dp[3][3] = dp[2][2]. From previous, dp[2][2]=1, so dp[3][3]=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — 2D DP Table Initialization and Iteration
ladders.desc: Initialize and iterate through a 2D DP table for string problems
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a 2D dynamic programming table to solve problems involving two sequences (like strings). We define a table dp with dimensions (n+1) x (m+1), where n and m are lengths of the sequences. The extra row and column (index 0) handle base cases involving empty prefixes. Initialization: set dp[i][0] = i for i from 0 to n (cost to transform first i characters of s to empty string by deletions), and dp[0][j] = j for j from 0 to m (cost to transform empty string to first j characters of t by insertions). Then, we iterate through the table row by row (or column by column) from i=1 to n and j=1 to m, filling each cell using a recurrence relation. The iteration order must ensure that when computing dp[i][j], the cells dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], and dp[i-1][j-1] are already computed; row-major order (i outer, j inner) works. The intuition is that we systematically build solutions from smallest subproblems (empty strings) to larger ones. Time complexity is O(n*m) for the nested loops. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the table. Connection to L2: This provides the framework to apply the recurrence relation across all subproblems. Common mistakes include incorrect table dimensions (forgetting +1), wrong initialization values, or iteration order that doesn't respect dependencies. Real code pattern: n, m = len(s), len(t); dp = [[0]*(m+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; for i in range(n+1): dp[i][0] = i; for j in range(m+1): dp[0][j] = j; for i in range(1, n+1): for j in range(1, m+1): # fill dp[i][j] using recurrence.

examples.trace: Create dp table with 3 rows (0,1,2) and 3 columns (0,1,2). Initialize: dp[0][0]=0, dp[1][0]=1, dp[2][0]=2; dp[0][1]=1, dp[0][2]=2. Table: [[0,1,2],[1,0,0],[2,0,0]] (zeros are placeholders).

testCases.expected: Table with 11x11, first column 0..10, first row 0..10, rest zeros

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison and Minimum of Three Values
ladders.desc: Compare characters and compute minimum of three numbers
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two fundamental tasks: comparing two characters for equality, and finding the minimum among three numbers. Character comparison is straightforward: given characters a and b, check if a == b. This is used in the DP recurrence to decide if no edit is needed. Finding the minimum of three numbers x, y, z involves computing min(x, y, z). In Python, this can be done with min(x, y, z). Alternatively, it can be computed stepwise: first min_xy = min(x, y), then min_all = min(min_xy, z). The intuition is that we need to pick the cheapest edit operation, which requires comparing costs. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in the DP table iteration to fill each cell. Common mistakes include using assignment instead of comparison for characters, or incorrect order in min function. Real code pattern: if s[i-1] == t[j-1]: # characters equal; else: cost = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]).

examples.trace: Compare 'a' and 'b': they are different, so False. Compute min(3,1,2): compare 3 and 1 → 1 is smaller; compare 1 and 2 → 1 is smaller; so min is 1.
Compare 'x' and 'x': they are equal, so True. Compute min(0,2,1): compare 0 and 2 → 0 is smaller; compare 0 and 1 → 0 is smaller; so min is 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand strings as sequences and access characters by index
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers basic string manipulation and array indexing. A string is a sequence of characters, e.g., 'hello' has characters 'h','e','l','l','o'. In programming, strings are often zero-indexed: the first character has index 0, second has index 1, etc. For a string s of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing s[i] returns the character at position i. The length can be obtained with len(s). An empty string '' has length 0 and no indices. This is crucial for DP because we need to access characters at specific positions when comparing. The intuition is that we treat strings as arrays to process them character by character. Connection to L4: This enables character comparison by accessing s[i-1] and t[j-1]. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using index n for length-n string), confusing length with indices, or not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: s = 'abc'; n = len(s); first_char = s[0] (value 'a'); last_char = s[n-1] (value 'c'); iterate with for i in range(n): char = s[i].

examples.trace: String 'algorithm' has indices: 0:'a',1:'l',2:'g',3:'o',4:'r',5:'i',6:'t',7:'h',8:'m'. Index 3 corresponds to character 'o'.

examples.input: s='data', get length and last character
examples.trace: String 'data' has characters: 0:'d',1:'a',2:'t',3:'a'. Length len(s)=4. Last character is at index 3: s[3]='a'.
